author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: You might also check into the clay content of the paper you are using. Different paper types, such as news print have more clay in the weave then ordinary book printing. Try this experiment with newspaper and see if your results differ. Again, great job.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text:
I’ve been preparing roofs and walls and ceilings for blowing in cellulose insulation for more than 15 years. 
Borax or ammonia were used as fire retarders. Borax is on the list of to be banned products. Not sure about ammonia which is also not really healthy. We also used hemp, sheep wool and wood flocs, the list of rather ecological insulation stuff is long but not well known to many people. Products are rated into categories varying from inflammable to highly flammable. Always look up the product sheet of a material you want to use and for which purpose it can serve.

author: @Gravel1331
text: When I was about age 10 (1978) a friend's brother was making a model rocket engine from scratch for a high school science project.  He used a cardboard tube as the casing for the motor.  I remember that he impregnated (by soaking and drying) the cardboard with a sodium bicarbonate solution as a fire retardant.  I have no idea where he came up with the idea, or if it worked.

author: @deslomeslager
text:
May I ask an off-topic question? The bi-carbonate made me think of it. I am using calcium carbonate to cover many of my window glasses. The sun is just too much on our well insulated house. The thing is: it does not stick well enough to the glass. It does a good job, but I am wondering: can I add something to it to make the chalk bind a tiny bit better to the glass? (the rain is slowly thinning the layer, until the sun is shining through, and i need to redo the layer).
PS: it works really VERY well, our house is so much cooler this summer! Such a cheap solution. And in the winter we can scrape it off, or use soap to remove it. Any how, I'd appreciate your help!

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
You could treat wallpaper with sugar and bicarbonate and improve your fire protection without large panels, though large rolls of paper are available as just that: wallpaper. So three to five layers of treated wallpaper would give you an inexpensive surface for future testing, especially if a flame retardant binder was used between the layers.

I wonder as well if this same principal can be applied to make an inexpensive peltier device due to the heat difference from front to back and having such a large surface to work with conductive ink on, how would that fare?

replies.author: @SephBane
replies.text: Dry wall is already pretty much fireproof, expect for its paper coating. Fire codes do a really good job already at protecting buildings. Your efforts would be better spent reducing possible fire sources/fuel inside the home or making a fire break outside. It takes fuel either inside or outside the a house to make a house easy to burn down.

replies.author: @ryanlebeck259
replies.text: @SephBane I was thinking more along the lines of a larger surface peltier device for atop a rocket stove demonstrating the topics found in this video, but thank you.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: One thing that everyone seems to miss is what plastic was available when Starlight was invented.  The only options that I could find from back then were variations of either ABS or Bake light.  Both have some similarities and properties that could end up as ' a mess " as a plastic, and wonderful as heat reflective paint.  Have you looked in to the ' at home ' making of bake light or ABS to see if there is a way to combine what you have shown so far and the methods of making either as a fire retardant plastic?

author: @McRootbeer
text: Maybe you could use the carbon foam from the homemade starlite as an insulator for the rocket stove and the Stirling engine.  If nighthawkinlight can make a forge that lasts multiple uses with it, then it should last a while insulating the outside of a woodstove.  You could make panels with paper or the putty, carbonize the first layer in the oven or with a blowtorch, and use it like firebrick. Or you could do it like paper mache.  It would be cool to see if this improves the stoves performance.

author: @Nighthawkinlight
text: I think you did a great job on the last video as well as this one. I would recommend erythritol or even better pentaerythritol instead of sugar. That's what commercial intumescents use for superior foaming, and rather than attracting insects it actually kills them if they eat it. It's safe for humans, not sure about various pets. Borax of course as an additive also kills insects, so that does make a sugar mix viable.

replies.author: @brandonfranklin4533

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thank you mate - I did watch your video on this - of course - who didn't lol - anyway I did ponder pentaerythritol but went with sugar as it is what most folks just have in their cupboard and I like the idea that folks might be inspired to just get up and try it for themselves and if the materials are handy they can easily give it a go and maybe investigate and improve from there - who knows we can all dream lol - anyway nice tip and thanks for sharing

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
I once had the bright idea to burn office paper shredding in my fire place to extend my fire wood heating.  It did not work.  Loosely placed shredded paper would actually not burn, and could even be used to smother the fire.  
Minuteman II ICBM re-entry vehicles used bonded cork as an ablative material.  The metal warhead casing of test launches were recovered from Johnson Island with almost no deformation.
Good video, even if it was a little Fahrenheit 451-ish.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - yeah the book burning bit was a worry lol - fascinating info mate - thanks for sharing

replies.author: @dansw0rkshop
replies.text: You can indeed make highly refractory crucibles from bread.  They work well until they burn away, which may be a long time if they're in a reducing atmosphere or inert gas shielded.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it kind of depends on what you want to do with it mate - if you want a fireproof decoration pasting you wall with burnt bread probably isn't the best idea - but if you want an insulating material - burnt bread is probably a whole lot better

author: @rogerbraintree9552
text: If one mashed the paper up into a mush and then mixed in golden syrup or molasses, this could make quite hard paper tiles.  It certainly gets on thinking, particularly in terms of heat insulation to make a really efficient brick with only the surface exposed for the sterling engine.  And what about heat insulating for cold water left out to cool over night, brought in as the cold surface for the sterling engine.  The possibilities are endless.

replies.author: @mrab4222
replies.text: At first I thought about paper pulp, but then I wondered whether toilet tissue would have enough fibres - it's certainly easier to make a pulp from it. The disadvantage would be that it wouldn't be waterproof.

replies.text: they are - there is a lot you could do with this

replies.author: @rogerbraintree9552
replies.text: @mrab4222 If you use a paper shredder the shreddings will make a good pulp.  If it's painted with temperature resistant coating it would make it waterproof.

author: @garethb5729
text:
I'm loving the fire series. Dude if I had any Novec spare I would send it you, without doubt. 
By majority BCF has been removed as a suppressant. By majority server centers use FM200 or hcf227ea 
Fm200 is the trade name, like hover. 
I mean Fm200 is crazy advanced but Novec is out of this world. 


About 10 years ago I came across a suppressant called 'cold fire' 
It was designed by an American. He had stomach ulcers. The chemical was designed to help his gut. It turned out that it was also heat resistant. I kid you not. I sprayed my arm with this 'cold fire' during a demonstration day and the blowtorch didn't even touch the hairs on my arm. I'm still shocked to this day. I have very little information about 'cold fire'. I will never forget that day though. I mean a blowtorch is like 2500°c or something. Not a har was touched. 
Saying that now. I think it was something to do with the fire triangle. They said it wasnt a triangle. It was an octahedron. I vaguely believe it was something to do with this. (I say vaguely because we still use the triangle in the Industry)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I never know about it - but I looked it up and wow - I can think of so many uses for that!

author: @simonpeggboard4004
text: Maurice ward was a polymer chemist, he pushed the hairdressing narrative to keep people off the scent.

replies.author: @simonpeggboard4004
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Apparently he was an amateur chemist and used to try and confuse people by spouting nonsense to throw them of the scent. I don't think his theory was very good but his practical knowledge was pretty good. He was an odd character for sure. On a side note more patents at ICI have been from informally trained people than trained. By an order of magnitude.

author: @gingernutpreacher

replies.author: @simonpeggboard4004
replies.text: His daughter used to perform the experiments with him and kept the formula after his death. The interesting thing was they torched an egg at 2,000C with the coating for 10 mins then picked it up and placed the burnt side on their  hand immediately. Apparently it was only warm. The egg was also uncooked. Blew my mind when I saw it in the 80's.

replies.author: @duncyy7471
replies.text: In this sentence its a saying. He blew the opportunity/ the (original) idea, and the original product will never get the market it ones could get.
